hook.text: August 1, 2012. Knight Capital deployed new trading code. In 45 minutes they lost $440 million.

buildup.text: Old debug code was left on one of eight servers. It bought high and sold low automatically.

discovery.text: The deployment had no verification step. Nobody checked that all eight servers matched.

twist.text: A kill switch existed but nobody knew about it. By the time they pulled the plug, damage was done.

climax.text: Knight Capital was rescued by investors but lost its independence. One deploy killed a firm.

punchline.text: Verify every deploy. A missing check cost $440 million.

quiz.question: What caused Knight Capital's $440 million loss?
options: Old debug code left on one server during deployment
A hacker who infiltrated their trading system
A natural disaster that destroyed their data center

hook.text: October 4, 2021. Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp vanished. All of them. For six hours.

buildup.text: A routine BGP config change withdrew Facebook's routes. The internet forgot how to find them.

discovery.text: Their DNS servers became unreachable. No DNS meant no login to internal tools to fix it.

twist.text: Engineers couldn't badge into data centers either. The badge system depended on the network.

climax.text: They had to physically access routers to restore BGP. Six hours of global silence followed.

punchline.text: Don't depend on yourself to fix yourself. Keep out-of-band access.

quiz.question: Why couldn't Facebook engineers quickly fix the 2021 outage?
options: Internal tools depended on the same network that was down
The code was too complex to understand
They lacked skilled engineers

hook.text: July 2, 2019. Cloudflare went down globally. CPU usage hit 100%. The cause: one bad regex.

buildup.text: A WAF rule update contained a regex with catastrophic backtracking. It consumed all CPU.

discovery.text: The regex was deployed globally at once with no canary phase. Every edge server was hit.

twist.text: They couldn't push a fix because the deployment system used the same network that was down.

climax.text: Engineers walked to the data center and applied the fix manually. Downtime: 27 minutes.

punchline.text: Test regex carefully. One bad pattern can take down a CDN.

quiz.question: What caused Cloudflare's 2019 global outage?
options: A regular expression with catastrophic backtracking
A distributed denial-of-service attack
A power failure at their main data center

hook.text: July 19, 2024. 8.5 million Windows PCs crashed at once. Airlines, banks, hospitals — all down.

buildup.text: CrowdStrike pushed a faulty sensor config update. It caused a kernel-level crash on boot.

discovery.text: The update skipped staged rollout. Every Windows machine with the agent got it at once.

twist.text: Machines couldn't boot. The fix required manual intervention on each individual computer.

climax.text: Airlines grounded flights. Hospitals canceled surgeries. The estimated cost: over $10 billion.

punchline.text: Canary your updates. Especially kernel-level ones.

quiz.question: Why was the CrowdStrike incident so devastating?
options: The update was pushed to all machines at once without staged rollout
It was a sophisticated cyberattack
The software was outdated and unpatched

hook.text: October 2021. Roblox went down for 73 hours. Millions of kids couldn't play for three days.

buildup.text: A minor config change to their service mesh caused a cascade of failures across Consul.

discovery.text: Consul's KV store became overwhelmed. Service discovery broke. Nothing could find anything.

twist.text: The root cause was subtle: a hash table contention bug amplified by the config change.

climax.text: It took three days because the bug was deep in the platform layer. Hard to find, hard to fix.

punchline.text: Platform bugs cascade the hardest. Invest in platform testing.

quiz.question: What made the Roblox outage so long?
options: The bug was deep in the platform layer and hard to diagnose
They lost all their source code
Their engineers were on vacation

hook.text: February 28, 2017. A typo in an AWS command took down S3. Half the internet broke.

buildup.text: An engineer ran a debug command to remove a few S3 servers. A typo removed far too many.

discovery.text: S3 in us-east-1 went down. Thousands of websites, apps, and IoT devices failed instantly.

twist.text: Even the AWS status dashboard was hosted on S3. It showed green while the world burned.

climax.text: AWS added safeguards: slower removal rates and minimum capacity thresholds. One typo changed policy.

punchline.text: Guard against fat-finger errors. Rate-limit destructive ops.

quiz.question: What caused the 2017 AWS S3 outage?
options: A typo in a command removed too many servers
A cyberattack on Amazon's infrastructure
A natural disaster at the data center

hook.text: June 2, 2019. Google Cloud, YouTube, and Gmail went down. A config change cascaded globally.

buildup.text: A network config change meant for one region was applied to multiple regions by mistake.

discovery.text: Traffic was routed to too few servers. Those servers overloaded and dropped requests.

twist.text: The misconfiguration spread because the safety system that should have blocked it had a bug.

climax.text: YouTube, Snapchat, and Discord all went down. Google's outage rippled across the internet.

punchline.text: Safety systems need testing too. Guard the guards.

quiz.question: Why did the Google Cloud 2019 outage spread so widely?
options: The safety system that should have prevented spread had a bug
Google had only one data center
A physical cable was cut

hook.text: June 8, 2021. Reddit, Twitch, and the UK government site went down. Fastly's CDN had failed.

buildup.text: A customer toggled a config setting that triggered a latent software bug in Fastly's network.

discovery.text: The bug had existed for weeks. The specific config combination that triggered it was rare.

twist.text: 85% of Fastly's network returned errors. A single customer action broke a global CDN.

climax.text: Fastly fixed it in 49 minutes. They later deployed a fix for the underlying latent bug.

punchline.text: Latent bugs wait for triggers. Test rare config combinations.

quiz.question: What triggered the Fastly CDN outage in 2021?
options: A customer config change that hit a latent software bug
A massive DDoS attack
A data center fire

hook.text: December 24, 2012. Families sat down to stream. Netflix was down. An AWS ELB issue was the cause.

buildup.text: Netflix depended on a single AWS region. When us-east-1 degraded, there was no fallback.

discovery.text: The single-region dependency was the single point of failure. No multi-region failover existed.

twist.text: After this, Netflix invested heavily in active-active multi-region architecture.

climax.text: They built Chaos Kong to simulate losing an entire region. Resilience became a core value.

punchline.text: One region is one point of failure. Go multi-region.

quiz.question: What key change did Netflix make after the 2012 outage?
options: They built active-active multi-region architecture
They switched away from AWS entirely
They stopped streaming on holidays

hook.text: May 2017. A power cut at BA's data center stranded 75,000 passengers for three days.

buildup.text: A contractor disconnected power. The uncontrolled restart surged and damaged servers.

discovery.text: Check-in, baggage, and flights all ran from one data center. One failure grounded everything.

twist.text: BA had a DR site but had never tested it at full load. It couldn't handle the demand.

climax.text: The outage cost BA over $100 million and triggered a massive regulatory fine.

punchline.text: Test your DR at full scale. Untested DR is a wish, not a plan.

quiz.question: Why did BA's disaster recovery site fail to help?
options: It had never been tested under full production load
It was in the same building as the primary site
The DR site's software was outdated

hook.text: A former AWS employee exploited a misconfigured firewall. 100 million records were stolen.

buildup.text: Capital One's WAF was misconfigured, allowing SSRF attacks against their cloud metadata.

discovery.text: The attacker accessed IAM credentials via the metadata service and read S3 buckets.

twist.text: The data had been in the cloud for years. The misconfiguration went undetected.

climax.text: AWS made IMDSv2 the default after this. Token-based metadata access is now standard.

punchline.text: Lock down cloud IAM. The shared responsibility model is real.

quiz.question: How did the attacker access Capital One's data?
options: Through a misconfigured firewall allowing SSRF to cloud metadata
By guessing an employee's password
By physically breaking into a data center

hook.text: A popular npm package got a new maintainer. That maintainer injected crypto-stealing malware.

buildup.text: The original author was tired and handed off ownership. No vetting of the new maintainer.

discovery.text: Malware hid inside a dependency of a dependency. It targeted one specific Bitcoin wallet app.

twist.text: It took weeks for the community to find the attack. Obfuscated code buried layers deep.

climax.text: The attack exposed fragility in open-source trust. Millions of packages depend on volunteers.

punchline.text: Audit dependency changes. Open-source trust is fragile.

quiz.question: How was malware introduced into the event-stream package?
options: A new maintainer added a malicious dependency
An automated bot modified the source code
npm's servers were hacked

hook.text: Russian hackers compromised SolarWinds' build pipeline. 18,000 organizations installed the trojan.

buildup.text: Attackers injected malware into the Orion software build process. Official updates carried it.

discovery.text: The malware was signed by SolarWinds' own certificates. It looked perfectly legitimate.

twist.text: US government agencies and Fortune 500 companies were all compromised via trusted updates.

climax.text: The attack went undetected for nine months. It redefined supply chain security thinking.

punchline.text: Secure your build pipeline. Signed doesn't mean safe.

quiz.question: What made the SolarWinds attack so effective?
options: Malware was injected into the official build pipeline and signed
Hackers guessed all the passwords
They exploited an unpatched zero-day in Windows

hook.text: In 2017, attackers wiped 28,000 MongoDB databases and demanded Bitcoin ransoms.

buildup.text: MongoDB's default install had no auth and bound to all interfaces. Databases were wide open.

discovery.text: Attackers scanned for open ports, connected, exported data, deleted it, and left a ransom note.

twist.text: Most victims who paid the ransom never got data back. Attackers often just deleted it.

climax.text: MongoDB changed defaults to localhost-only binding. But thousands of open instances remain.

punchline.text: Secure defaults matter. Authentication is not optional.

quiz.question: Why were so many MongoDB databases vulnerable?
options: Default installation had no authentication and was publicly accessible
MongoDB had a known zero-day exploit
Users forgot to update their software

hook.text: December 2021. A zero-day in Log4j affected millions of Java apps worldwide. Severity: 10 out of 10.

buildup.text: Log4j is a logging library used everywhere. The bug allowed remote code execution via a log message.

discovery.text: Attackers could send a crafted string in a header. If it was logged, the server was compromised.

twist.text: Many teams didn't even know they used Log4j. It was buried deep in their dependency trees.

climax.text: The scramble to patch took weeks. SBOM adoption surged as teams realized they needed visibility.

punchline.text: Know your dependencies. An SBOM isn't optional anymore.

quiz.question: Why was the Log4Shell vulnerability so dangerous?
options: Log4j was used in millions of applications, many unknowingly
It only affected a single company
It required physical access to exploit

hook.text: January 2017. A tired GitLab engineer ran rm -rf on production. 300 GB of data vanished.

buildup.text: They were fixing replication late at night. They typed the wrong directory path.

discovery.text: Five backup systems existed. None worked. All were misconfigured or empty.

twist.text: They recovered from a 6-hour-old staging snapshot — the only working copy anywhere.

climax.text: Six hours of user data were lost permanently. GitLab shared the full story publicly.

punchline.text: Test your backups. Untested backups are not backups.

quiz.question: What critical lesson came from GitLab's database deletion?
options: Backups must be tested regularly to ensure they work
Engineers should never work on databases
Cloud providers handle all backup needs automatically

hook.text: In 2019, MySpace admitted 50 million songs from 2003-2015 were lost in a server migration.

buildup.text: During the migration, audio files were corrupted or deleted. No one noticed right away.

discovery.text: Users found their music missing. MySpace blamed a migration issue. The data was gone forever.

twist.text: Early recordings of now-famous artists and rare demos were lost. Irreplaceable cultural data.

climax.text: No verified backup existed. Twelve years of music history vanished in one botched migration.

punchline.text: Verify backups before you migrate. Test the restore path.

quiz.question: What happened to MySpace's music during the server migration?
options: 50 million songs were lost because backups were not verified
The music was moved to a new platform successfully
Users chose to delete their own music

hook.text: Someone ran rm -rf on Toy Story 2 at Pixar. 90% of the movie vanished from the servers.

buildup.text: Characters started disappearing. Woody's hat, then his boots, then entire scenes.

discovery.text: They pulled the plug on servers to stop it. Then they checked backups. Backups were broken.

twist.text: A director on maternity leave had a full copy on her home computer. The only surviving copy.

climax.text: They drove her machine to the studio wrapped in blankets. The movie was saved from one laptop.

punchline.text: Verify backups actively. Luck saved Toy Story 2.

quiz.question: How was Toy Story 2 ultimately saved?
options: An employee had a copy on their home computer
Pixar's cloud backup was restored
They rebuilt the movie from scratch in two weeks

hook.text: October 2018. GitHub was degraded for 24 hours. A routine network change triggered MySQL chaos.

buildup.text: A brief network partition caused the orchestrator to promote a replica to primary.

discovery.text: Two databases thought they were primary. Writes went to both. Data diverged.

twist.text: GitHub chose data integrity over speed. They stayed degraded while reconciling all data.

climax.text: 24 hours of degradation but zero data lost. The postmortem was praised for transparency.

punchline.text: Automated failover can split-brain. Test for partitions.

quiz.question: How did GitHub handle the split-brain situation?
options: They prioritized data integrity over quick recovery
They rolled back all changes from both databases
They shut down GitHub permanently

hook.text: February 2024. Slack went degraded for hours. A database migration caused cascading locks.

buildup.text: Schema changes on large tables locked rows. Millions of queries queued behind the locks.

discovery.text: The migration worked fine in staging. Staging lacked production-level traffic to reveal the issue.

twist.text: Dependent services timed out. Their callers timed out. A tree of failures grew from one table.

climax.text: Slack rolled back the migration and restored service. They added load-testing to future plans.

punchline.text: Test migrations under real load. Staging lies about scale.

quiz.question: Why did Slack's database migration fail in production?
options: Staging didn't have production-level traffic to expose locking issues
The schema change had a syntax error
Slack forgot to back up their database

hook.text: September 2015. DynamoDB slowed in us-east-1. Services retried. The retries made it worse.

buildup.text: A metadata partition overloaded. Tables became briefly unavailable. Clients started retrying.

discovery.text: Retries created more load. More load caused more failures. A feedback loop amplified the problem.

twist.text: Services without timeouts waited forever, consuming all threads. They died silently.

climax.text: AWS redesigned the metadata system. Customers learned: add timeouts and circuit breakers.

punchline.text: Retries without backoff amplify failures. Add jitter.

quiz.question: What amplified the DynamoDB outage into a cascade?
options: Aggressive client retries created a feedback loop
A physical hardware failure destroyed the servers
An attacker overwhelmed the service with traffic

hook.text: 2007-2009. Twitter's Fail Whale was shown so often it became a pop culture icon.

buildup.text: Twitter was built on Ruby on Rails for a small user base. Then it went viral.

discovery.text: Every tweet fanned out to all followers. Popular accounts caused massive write amplification.

twist.text: Twitter rebuilt everything. Ruby became Scala. Monolith became microservices. Years of work.

climax.text: They built Manhattan, Finagle, and custom caching. The Fail Whale finally disappeared.

punchline.text: Architecture must evolve with scale. Plan for growth.

quiz.question: What fundamental problem caused Twitter's Fail Whale?
options: The original architecture couldn't handle the viral growth in users
Twitter's servers were physically too small
The domain name expired repeatedly

hook.text: April 2022. Heroku discovered stolen OAuth tokens. They revoked all GitHub integrations.

buildup.text: Attackers accessed a Heroku database containing OAuth tokens for GitHub integrations.

discovery.text: The tokens let attackers access private repos of Heroku customers. Scope of compromise was unclear.

twist.text: Heroku's incident communication was criticized. Updates were vague and slow for weeks.

climax.text: Customers lost trust. Many migrated away. Transparent communication could have limited the damage.

punchline.text: Communicate clearly during incidents. Silence erodes trust.

quiz.question: What worsened the impact of Heroku's 2022 security incident?
options: Vague and slow communication during the incident
The attack affected all cloud providers
Heroku's source code was deleted

hook.text: June 2022. A BGP misconfiguration routed Cloudflare traffic through the wrong networks.

buildup.text: A change in 19 data centers triggered a broader BGP issue. Traffic was misrouted globally.

discovery.text: Cloudflare sites went offline or slowed. Users saw 500 errors across many popular websites.

twist.text: The fix was deployed in 87 minutes. But the incident showed how fragile internet routing is.

climax.text: Cloudflare improved their change procedures and added BGP prefix validation checks.

punchline.text: BGP is fragile. One wrong route can break the internet.

quiz.question: What fundamental internet protocol was involved in Cloudflare's 2022 issue?
options: BGP, the protocol that routes traffic between networks
HTTP, the protocol for web requests
DNS, the domain name resolution protocol

hook.text: March 2021. A fire destroyed OVHcloud's SBG2 data center. 3.6 million websites went down.

buildup.text: The fire started in a UPS battery room and spread fast. The entire building was destroyed.

discovery.text: Many customers had backups stored in the same facility. When it burned, backups burned too.

twist.text: Some customers lost data permanently. Their only copies were in the destroyed data center.

climax.text: The incident proved that geographic redundancy isn't optional. Off-site backups are essential.

punchline.text: Store backups off-site. Same building is not redundancy.

quiz.question: Why did some OVHcloud customers lose data permanently?
options: Their backups were stored in the same data center that burned
OVHcloud didn't offer any backup services
The fire was caused by a cyberattack

hook.text: Google, Etsy, and Netflix publish incident reports. Transparency helps the entire industry.

buildup.text: Public postmortems let everyone learn from one company's mistake. Patterns don't repeat.

discovery.text: Great postmortems include timeline, root cause, impact, and specific action items.

twist.text: Companies that hide incidents repeat them. Those that share improve faster.

climax.text: Blameless culture is key. If people fear punishment, they hide failures. Learning stops.

punchline.text: Share failures openly. The industry learns together.

quiz.question: Why do companies like Google and Netflix share postmortems publicly?
options: To help the entire industry learn from incidents
They are legally required to
To embarrass their competitors

hook.text: Every disaster in this course had one thing in common: one layer failed and nothing caught it.

buildup.text: Defense in depth uses multiple safety layers. If one fails, the next catches the problem.

discovery.text: Code review, CI tests, canary deploys, and monitoring form layers of protection.

twist.text: No single layer is perfect. Each catches different problems. The combination is the strength.

climax.text: Knight Capital lacked deploy checks. GitLab lacked backup tests. Each missed one layer.

punchline.text: Layer your defenses. Assume each will eventually fail.

quiz.question: What is the core principle of defense in depth?
options: Using multiple independent safety layers that catch different problems
Having one perfect security system
Hiring more security engineers

hook.text: The GitLab engineer was tired. The Knight team rushed. Humans are part of every system.

buildup.text: Fatigue, time pressure, and unclear interfaces increase human error rates.

discovery.text: Instead of blaming humans, design systems that tolerate mistakes. Add confirmations and undo.

twist.text: Dangerous commands should need the most confirmation. 'Delete production' needs more than Enter.

climax.text: Netflix requires peer review for production changes. Good systems protect against lone mistakes.

punchline.text: Design for human error. Make the system forgiving.

quiz.question: How should systems handle human error?
options: Design systems that tolerate mistakes with confirmations and undo
Fire engineers who make mistakes
Remove all human access to production

hook.text: GitLab assumed backups worked. BA assumed DR would failover. Assumptions kill reliability.

buildup.text: We assume backups run, failover works, and rollback is fast. But do we verify?

discovery.text: Restore from backup regularly. Fail over to DR periodically. Test rollback before you need it.

twist.text: Game days and chaos engineering exist to test assumptions. Break things before they break you.

climax.text: Write down your critical assumptions. Then test each one. Schedule it. Track the results.

punchline.text: An untested assumption is a risk in disguise.

quiz.question: What is the best way to validate operational assumptions?
options: Test them regularly through drills and chaos engineering
Trust that they work based on the initial setup
Wait until a real incident reveals the truth

hook.text: Netflix and Google don't avoid all failures. They recover faster because reliability is cultural.

buildup.text: Technology alone can't prevent disasters. Culture determines how teams respond and learn.

discovery.text: Blameless postmortems, error budgets, and chaos engineering are cultural practices.

twist.text: The most reliable companies celebrate near-misses. A caught failure is a learning moment.

climax.text: Reliability is a team sport. Every engineer, every deploy, every review adds a layer of safety.

punchline.text: Build the culture. Reliability follows the people.

quiz.question: What is the foundation of reliable systems?
options: A culture that learns from failures and celebrates near-misses
The most expensive infrastructure available
Hiring only the most experienced engineers
